brief_summary: Four stacked cross-section diagrams of canonical mount styles. Top: gasket-mount (plate sandwiched between bordeaux foam strips, floating). Second: top-mount (plate bolted via bordeaux screw markers to top case standoffs). Third: tray-mount (top case dashed-decorative; PCB screws to bottom standoffs with bordeaux markers at intervals). Bottom: integrated plate (top case and plate as one continuous bordeaux-outlined piece). Each panel has a right-margin mechanical-label callout and a left-margin character note.
data_sources: mounting-styles-compared.mdx § 'Gasket-mount' — 'Gasket-mount sandwiches strips of foam ... between the plate and the case shell on both top and bottom. The plate does not touch the case directly.'
mounting-styles-compared.mdx § 'Top-mount' — 'Top-mount screws the plate directly to standoffs on the inside of the top case half.'

mounting-styles-compared.mdx § 'Tray-mount and the no plate fixing family' — 'The PCB screws directly to standoffs on the bottom case shell, and the plate ... clips into the switches with no mechanical link to either case half.'
mounting-styles-compared.mdx § 'Integrated plate' — 'Integrated plate machines the plate as part of the case rather than as a separate piece. There is no plate-to-case seam.'
